seed: batch-03 passage B; epistemic flattening (G3), lost contestation and delay
passage: When the central bank raises the policy rate, borrowing becomes more expensive, so firms and households cut credit-financed spending. Higher rates also tend to strengthen the currency, which makes imports cheaper and can dampen inflation directly. The effect on investment is less clear-cut and operates with long and variable lags — some estimates place the peak impact at 12–18 months. There is also an expectations channel that many economists judge at least as important as the direct cost-of-borrowing effect, though its magnitude is hard to pin down.
